title: The Strong Do What They Can—and Suffer What They Must
gist: Political scientist Jonathan Kirshner rebuts the Trump administration's and Niall Ferguson's invocation of Thucydides's Melian Dialogue as an endorsement of 'might makes right,' arguing the actual lesson of The Peloponnesian War is that unrestrained power and hubris — culminating in the disastrous Sicilian expedition — destroy the strong rather than vindicate them, a direct historical warning against the current White House's 'iron laws of power' rhetoric.

themes.theme: Trump-era invocations of the 'iron laws of power'

insights.claim: Trump described the 2025-2026 capture of Venezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, along with strikes on alleged Caribbean drug smugglers and the bombing of Iran, as vindication of 'iron laws that have always determined global power,' explicitly organizing his foreign policy around a raw-power doctrine.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Signals an administration explicitly organizing foreign policy around a raw-power doctrine, raising the risk premium on U.S. actions against weaker states and allies.
insights.quote: iron laws that have always determined global power.

insights.claim: White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller stated in January 2026 that the world is 'governed by force' and 'governed by power,' calling these 'the iron laws of the world since the beginning of time.'
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Confirms the raw-power doctrine is a stated organizing principle at the highest levels of the administration, not a one-off comment, reinforcing the pattern for policy and geopolitical-risk forecasting.
insights.quote: these are the iron laws of the world since the beginning of time.
insights.locator: White House Deputy Chief of Staff Stephen Miller asserted in January

insights.claim: Historian Niall Ferguson's essay 'How Trump Won Davos' explicitly invoked the Melian Dialogue to celebrate Trump as a triumphant realist, asserting the realists 'won an emphatic victory' at Melos — a reading Kirshner argues inverts Thucydides's actual intent.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific intellectual claim Kirshner's essay is written to rebut — useful for tracking the 'Thucydidean realism' narrative gaining currency in elite commentary around the Trump administration.
insights.quote: the realists won an emphatic victory.
insights.locator: In “How Trump Won Davos,” an essay published in January

insights.claim: Kirshner argues that Thucydides never endorses the 'might makes right' reading attributed to him; the real lesson of The Peloponnesian War is that the unchecked ambition of the strong leads to their own undoing, not a celebration of strength.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: The essay's central interpretive thesis — recasts a doctrine currently used to justify aggressive U.S. foreign policy as a historically discredited misreading, relevant for gauging pushback against 'might makes right' policy framing.
insights.locator: But that understanding of both the dialogue and its author

insights.claim: Kirshner distinguishes the popular 'Thucydides trap' (Graham Allison's term for structural war between a rising and an incumbent power) from what he calls the real trap: Athens's shift from Pericles's prudent strategy to reckless overextension in the Sicilian expedition, which caused its downfall.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Reframes a widely-used China-U.S. analytical tool (Allison's 'Thucydides trap'); readers citing that concept in a China-competition context should note the author disputes its core mechanism.
insights.locator: The political scientist Graham Allison famously coined

themes.theme: The Melian Dialogue as deliberate narrative device

insights.claim: The Melian campaign had no strategic or military significance to the war's outcome, yet Thucydides devotes several pages of dialogue to it — the only extended dialogue in the entire work — a technique one scholar calls 'extreme narrative deceleration.'
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Signals that length and emphasis in classical (or any) texts encode authorial intent, not factual importance — a reading-comprehension flag relevant to how policymakers cite historical 'lessons.'
insights.locator: Thucydides deploys the tactic of what one scholar has called

insights.claim: Five years before Melos, Athens committed a nearly identical atrocity at Scione — killing the men, enslaving the women and children — but Thucydides gave it barely a sentence, proof that the extended Melos treatment was a deliberate authorial choice, not proportionate reporting of severity.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Reinforces that Melos is a constructed parable, strengthening the case against reading it as a literal endorsement of might-makes-right.
insights.quote: Athens succeeded in reducing Scione, put the adult males to death and, making slaves of the women and children, gave the land to the Plataeans to live in.
insights.locator: the Athenians had done what they did on Melos before

insights.claim: Thucydides admits the dialogue's speeches are reconstructed to convey 'the general sense of what they really said' rather than verbatim transcripts, and the Athenian speakers at Melos are left unnamed while no Melian participants survived to give an account, meaning the dialogue reflects Thucydides's own invention as much as historical record.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Undercuts any literal, doctrinal reading of the Melian Dialogue as reported fact rather than authorial argument.
insights.quote: adhering as closely as possible to the general sense of what they really said
insights.locator: Thucydides explains that he often imagines encounters

insights.claim: Thucydides places the destruction of Melos immediately before his account of the disastrous Athenian invasion of Sicily, explicitly linking Athenian arrogance at Melos to the hubris that produced Athens's catastrophic defeat in Sicily.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: The essay's core causal chain — unchecked displays of power (Melos) directly precede and foreshadow strategic overreach and defeat (Sicily) — the actionable historical analogy for assessing current U.S. 'iron laws' rhetoric.
insights.quote: The same winter the Athenians resolved to sail again to Sicily . . . if possible to conquer the island.
insights.locator: Immediately after describing the annihilation of Melos

insights.claim: A decade before Melos, Athens debated annihilating Mytilene for attempting to defect to Sparta; the assembly initially voted for total massacre under the demagogue Cleon but reversed itself the next morning over 'the horrid cruelty' of the decree, sending a second ship to rescind the order in time.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Establishes the baseline of Athenian restraint that later erodes — the evidentiary basis for Kirshner's 'corruption of Athens' thesis.
insights.quote: the horrid cruelty of a decree which condemned a whole city to the fate merited only by the guilty.

insights.claim: By the time of Melos, over a decade after Mytilene, Athens shows no such reconsideration and faced no comparable provocation (Melos merely wanted neutrality), evidence that prolonged war had coarsened Athenian society and eroded its capacity for restraint.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A generalizable claim about how protracted conflict degrades a power's own norms — a relevant lens for assessing multi-year conflicts for creeping escalation.
insights.locator: the sack of Melos shows how Athenian society had coarsened

insights.claim: Thucydides dwells at length on atrocities beyond Melos, including a civil-war massacre on Corfu, 'lawless extravagance' during the Athens plague, and the Thracian mercenaries' massacre of a boys' school at Mycalessus, showing a recurring preoccupation with how war disfigures civilization.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Broadens the evidentiary base beyond Melos alone, showing the 'war corrupts societies' theme is systemic in the text rather than a one-off reading.
insights.quote: sacked the houses and the temples and butchered the inhabitants, sparing neither youth nor age
insights.locator: details of an otherwise inconsequential frenzied rampage

insights.claim: Kirshner's central argument is that Thucydides did not believe unrestrained violence serves a strong state's interests, contradicting the modern invocation — e.g., Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth's calls to 'untie' the hands of warriors and ignore 'stupid' rules of engagement — of Thucydides as a patron of ruthless power politics.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts a rules-of-engagement-loosening posture in current U.S. defense policy by appeal to the same classical source cited to justify it — useful for gauging elite pushback on unrestrained-force doctrine.
insights.quote: pay no heed to norms, laws, or “stupid” rules of engagement
insights.locator: as U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth has urged

insights.claim: The Melians warned the Athenians that annihilating them would set a dangerous precedent turning all existing neutral states into future enemies; Kirshner notes the Melians 'utterly routed the Athenians in the debate,' a warning later validated when Sparta's allies called for Athens's own annihilation at war's end.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Illustrates a recurring geopolitical mechanic — maximal punitive force against weak actors converts neutrals into adversaries — a durable framework for evaluating great-power coercion campaigns.
insights.quote: How can you avoid making enemies of all existing neutrals who shall look at our case and conclude from it that one day or another you will attack them?
insights.locator: How can you avoid making enemies of all existing neutrals

insights.claim: At war's end, Xenophon records that Sparta's allies pushed for Athens's total destruction, and the Athenians 'feared that there was nothing that could save them from suffering the same evils that they themselves had inflicted' on smaller states, the precise reversal the Melians had predicted.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: Closes the causal loop empirically — the predicted blowback from Melian-style brutality materialized for Athens itself, the essay's strongest evidentiary payoff.
insights.quote: feared that there was nothing that could save them from suffering the same evils that they themselves had inflicted against the citizens of smaller states
insights.locator: As Xenophon, a contemporary who picked up Thucydides’s narrative

insights.claim: Thucydides shows unrestrained brutality is strategically counterproductive on both sides of the war — Spartan general Pausanias's brutal conduct drove Athens's allies away and toward Athens early in the war, while later Athenian imperial brutality flipped Greek sympathy toward Sparta.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: A symmetric historical case that excessive coercion erodes alliance cohesion regardless of which power wields it — applicable to assessing any great power's current coalition-management risk.
insights.quote: the hatred which he inspired had induced the allies to desert him . . . and to range themselves by the side of the Athenians.
insights.locator: the blood-soaked rampages of the Spartan general Pausanias

insights.claim: Kirshner closes by directly rebutting Trump's claim that 'I think God is very proud of the job I've done,' arguing Thucydides — who shows no reverence for divine sanction of power — would have viewed such boundless self-regard as exactly the hubris that precedes a great power's downfall.
insights.speaker: Jonathan Kirshner
insights.so_what: The essay's closing framing directly warns that current rhetoric of self-vindicated, unaccountable U.S. power (echoing Athens pre-Sicily) is a historical red flag for policy overreach, not a sign of strength.
insights.quote: I think God is very proud of the job I’ve done.
insights.locator: Looking back on the first year of his second term
